coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of eggshell waste in the preparation of carbon quantum dot nanoprobes for the determination of COVID-19 antiviral drug; molnupiravir
coredata.dc:creator: Kannouma, Reham E.
Kamal, Amira H.
Hammad, Mohamed A.
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: New carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with multi-doped elements from eggshell biomass waste have been employed as a direct, fast, green, and selective probe for the fluorometric determination of molnupiravir, a COVID-19 antiviral drug. For the first time, the eggshell@CQDs were prepared through a simple, single-step microwave heating process that only took 90 s. Upon excitation at λex 340 nm, the resulting CQDs exhibited a maximum bluish fluorescence emission at λem 408 nm. Molnupiravir was found to quench the native fluorescence of the prepared CQDs in a quantitative manner, based on a static mechanism by forming a non-emissive complex. The spectrofluorometric method designed for molnupiravir detection was validated per the International Council for Harmonization (ICH) requirements. The proposed fluorescent probe showed good linearity for molnupiravir detection over a range of 2.5–70 µg/mL, with percent recoveries ranging from 99.53 % to 101.79 % and %RSD (relative standard deviation) less than 1 %. The eggshell@CQDs were successfully employed to determine molnupiravir levels in marketed hard capsules, yielding good percent recoveries and repeatability. The developed approach was found to be greener than the published HPLC/UV approach, as assessed by the analytical eco-scale, green analytical procedure index (GAPI), and analytical greenness metric approach (AGREE). Furthermore, this approach highlights the potential of synthesizing CQDs from natural biomass, specifically low-value biomass waste such as eggshells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eggshell
Microwave synthesis
Green chemistry
Carbon quantum dots
Molnupiravir
COVID-19
Antiviral drug

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of molnupiravir in pharmaceutical capsules
Greenness evaluation and comparison of the developed approach to the previously reported HPLC approach

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the Main Chemical Lab (Cairo, Egypt) for their assistance with the characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of CQDs and application for molnupiravir estimation.

Emission fluorescence spectra of CQDs synthesized from different precursors: Glycerol (black), Eggshell/Glycerol (red), EDTA/Glycerol (green) and EDTA/Eggshell/Glycerol (blue). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
A) The HRTEM image of the synthesized CQDs under a magnification power of 50 nm (inset: SAED pattern of the CQDs). B) FTIR spectrum of the synthesized CQDs. C) XPS survey spectrum of the CQDs.
Emission fluorescence spectra after the addition of serial molnupiravir concentrations (0–200 μg/mL) to CQDs (inset: calibration graph).

The green analytical procedure index (GAPI) of the proposed method (A) and the reported method (B). The analytical greenness metric approach (AGREE) of the proposed method (C) and the reported method (D). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Assessment of accuracy, intraday and inter-day precision of the proposed approach for the estimation of molnupiravir.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Average of triplicate determinations, RSD: relative standard deviation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed approach for the estimation of molnupiravir in pharmaceutical dosage form and comparison with the reported HPLC approach.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Average of triplicate determinations, RSD: relative standard deviation.
a the tabulated values of t-test at p = 0.05.
b the tabulated values of F test at p = 0.05.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different reported methods for determination of molnupiravir with the proposed method.

row.entry: In Vitro Dissolution Study, Pharmaceutical Dosage Forms

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: TLC: thin layer chromatography; RP-HPLC: reversed-phase high-performance liquid chromatography.

head.ce:title: Incorporation of eggshell waste in the preparation of carbon quantum dot nanoprobes for the determination of COVID-19 antiviral drug; molnupiravir

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Sadat City, Sadat City, Monufia 32958, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Sadat City, Sadat City, Monufia 32958, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: The medical campus of Tanta University, Elgeish Street, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The medical campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: New carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with multi-doped elements from eggshell biomass waste have been employed as a direct, fast, green, and selective probe for the fluorometric determination of molnupiravir, a COVID-19 antiviral drug. For the first time, the eggshell@CQDs were prepared through a simple, single-step microwave heating process that only took 90 s. Upon excitation at λex 340 nm, the resulting CQDs exhibited a maximum bluish fluorescence emission at λem 408 nm. Molnupiravir was found to quench the native fluorescence of the prepared CQDs in a quantitative manner, based on a static mechanism by forming a non-emissive complex. The spectrofluorometric method designed for molnupiravir detection was validated per the International Council for Harmonization (ICH) requirements. The proposed fluorescent probe showed good linearity for molnupiravir detection over a range of 2.5–70 µg/mL, with percent recoveries ranging from 99.53 % to 101.79 % and %RSD (relative standard deviation) less than 1 %. The eggshell@CQDs were successfully employed to determine molnupiravir levels in marketed hard capsules, yielding good percent recoveries and repeatability. The developed approach was found to be greener than the published HPLC/UV approach, as assessed by the analytical eco-scale, green analytical procedure index (GAPI), and analytical greenness metric approach (AGREE). Furthermore, this approach highlights the potential of synthesizing CQDs from natural biomass, specifically low-value biomass waste such as eggshells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In late 2019, the COVID-19 pandemic surfaced as an acute respiratory disease with around 300 million patients worldwide and 5.4 million documented fatalities [1]. Patients over 65 years of age are the most susceptible group, with hospitalized and non-hospitalized patient fatality rates of 6.26 % and 3.5 %, respectively [2]. Even though established vaccinations have a significant impact on slowing the development of COVID-19, antiviral drugs remain necessary to treat infected patients [3,4]. A newly developed orally bioavailable ribonucleotide antiviral medication called molnupiravir has shown promise in lowering hospitalizations and fatalities in cases with moderate COVID-19. Molnupiravir is metabolized by the body into the active form. Once it enters the cell, it is converted into a molecule that resembles RNA. This RNA-like molecule is coupled with the virus genome by RNA polymerase in the following stage, leading to several alterations and mutations in the virus genome, preventing replication [5]. Considering that the detection of molnupiravir in the presence of its active metabolite or other antiviral medications has been presented using LC-MS/MS [6,7] and HPLC/UV [8], it is regrettable that improper disposal of organic solvents can cause environmental pollution and pose hazards to analysts when used in LC-MS/MS and HPLC. According to one study, a typical chromatographic process can produce up to 1 L of organic waste per day, resulting in millions of litres of toxic waste annually. In light of these harmful impacts, more environmentally friendly analytical techniques should be established to replace traditional methods and mitigate their detrimental effects [9,10].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) represent an innovative kind of carbon-based zero-dimensional substance that has received a lot of attention due to their perfect photostability, favourable biocompatibility, negligible toxicity, excellent water solubility, outstanding selectivity and sensitivity for analytes, adaptable fluorescence emission and excitation, high quantum yield, and large Stokes’ shifts [11]. CQDs are brilliant nanostructures with a wide range of uses in industrial and biomedical research. In the last few years, significant advancements have been made in the synthesis, features, and practical applications of CQDs. However, many researchers underestimate the significance of carbon sources, focusing solely on the characteristics and uses of CQDs.

Biomass is an excellent carbon source for CQD synthesis. Compared to other carbon sources, biomass carbon sources are environmentally beneficial natural resources with several advantages, such as being cheap, readily available, abundant, and green. Additionally, low-value biomass waste can be transformed into valuable and practical materials through the synthesis of CQDs from natural biomass. Unlike CQDs from artificial carbon sources, which require the inclusion of external heteroatoms, biomass with heteroatoms is the optimum starting material for CQD synthesis [12].

This study aims to develop a fluorescent probe that is selective, economical, and simpler to use than other organic fluorophores. In this study, chicken eggshell was used as a popular food waste for obtaining luminescent CQDs. Previous studies employed the inner eggshell membrane for synthesizing CQDs [13–17]. However, the abundance of eggshells offers an advantage over using the inner membrane. Moreover, the eggshell was found to contain several elements [18] that could be doped into the prepared CQDs, thereby enhancing their photoluminescence properties. This work presents the practical application of innovative, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly eggshell@CQDs for spectrofluorometric measurement of molnupiravir in pharmaceutical capsules. The eggshell@CQDs were prepared through a rapid and simple microwave-assisted pyrolysis method, taking only 90 s. The synthesis involved using ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (as a carbon and nitrogen source), chicken eggshell (as a source of sulfur, calcium, and phosphorus), and glycerol as a solvent.

UV/Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a double-beam UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific model Evolution 201, USA) connected to a PC loaded by Thermo Scientific Insight Pro software. All fluorescence measures were obtained using a JASCO FP-8300 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) with 5 nm slit widths and a scanning speed of 1000 nm/min and medium sensitivity equipped with a 1-cm quartz cell. Data processing and acquisition were obtained with the aid of Spectra Manager software (Jasco Co., Tokyo, Japan). The morphology and particle size of eggshell@CQDs, as well as the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern, were measured using a Thermo Scientific™ Talos F200i (S)TEM field emission (scanning) transmission electron microscope (Themo Fisher Scientific, USA)witha 200 kV acceleration voltage. The surface functioning of eggshell@CQDswas analyzed using the Burker Tensor 27FT/IR spectrophotometer (Burker, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) image of eggshell@CQDs was collected on Thermo Scientific™ K-Alpha™ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) System (Themo Fisher Scientific, USA). Tornado Microwave oven (900 W) model TM-25MK was used for CQDs synthesis. The pH measurements were performed using a Jenway 3510 pH meter of Cole-Parmer (Saint Neots, UK). An ultrasonic cleaning unit (Elmasonic- S 60H, Singen, Germany). Water was deionized using the SUEZ purification system (Paris, France).

Chicken eggs were bought from a local market. Molnupiravir (99.80 %) were kindly supplied by Eva Pharma Company (Cairo, Egypt). Phosphoric acid was purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid disodium salt (EDTA), Glycerol, sodium hydroxide, acetic acid, boric acid, mannitol, sorbitol, sucrose, fructose, glucose, lactose, carboxy methyl cellulose (CMC), hydroxy propyl methyl cellulose (HPMC), and magnesium stearate were purchased from Piochem company (6th of October, Egypt). Molnupiravir Eva Pharma® hard capsules with batch no. 2207627 (Eva Pharma Co., Cairo, Egypt). Each capsule is labelled to contain 200 mg molnupiravir. Deionized water was used for the preparation of aqueous solutions.

The outer shell of a freshly boiled chicken egg was cleaned and washed with deionized water. The inner shell membrane was removed and discarded. After drying, the eggshell was ground into very fine particles using a mortar. For the synthesis of eggshell@CQDs, 0.7 g of EDTA and 1.32 g of a ground shell were dissolved in 20 mL of glycerol before sonication for 10 min. The mixture was heated up in the microwave oven (high power,900 W) for 90 s. 20 mL deionized water was added to the formed orange syrup and sonicated for 5 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. This working solution was stored in the refrigerator until use, and it was stable for more than 1 month with no precipitation. Fluorescence intensities of this solution were measured at λem = 408 nm after being excited at λex = 340 nm.

The QY of the eggshell@CQDs was determined using the single-point method (Eq. (1)) [19].
(1)
Q
Y CQD
=
Q
Y
ST
X
(
F CQD
/
F ST
) X ( A
ST
/ A
CQD
) X
(
η CQD
/
η ST
)
2

Where F is the integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance and η is the refractive index of the solvent. Quinine sulfate (QS) was used as the standard. QS was dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid (QYst was 0.54 at 350 nm). The absorbance value was set at 0.05 to standardize the absorbance impact. In an aqueous solution, the ηCQD/ ηST = 1.

The stock solution of molnupiravir was prepared by dissolving 10 mg in 10 mL of deionized water to get a concentration of 1 mg/mL. Different volumes of the stock solutions were added to 200 µL of eggshell@CQDs and completed the volume to 10 mL with deionized water to obtain final molnupiravir concentrations in the range of 2.5–70 μg/mL. Fluorescence emission intensities were measured atλem = 408 nm after being excited at λex = 340 nm against the blank. The blank was obtained in a 10 mL volumetric flask by adding 200 μL eggshell@CQDs and adjusting to the volume with deionized water. The fluorescence quenching values (F°-F) were plotted against the corresponding drug concentrations (μg/mL) to obtain the calibration curve.

Ten capsules of Molnupiravir Eva Pharma® were evacuated and weighed accurately. An accurately weighed portion of the powder equivalent to 200 mg of molnupiravir was dissolved in deionized water by sonication for 20 min and the volume was completed to 100 mL (2 mg/mL). The obtained stock solution was filtered, and a specified volume of the filtrate was added to 200 μL eggshell@CQDs and adjusted the volume to 10 mL with the deionized water to obtain the final working solutions. The percentage recoveries were calculated and the findings were compared to the percentage recoveries acquired viathe previously mentioned HPLC method. [8].

Fig. 1 shows the steps of eggshell@CQDs preparation. Carbon quantum dots synthesis was first investigated using an aqueous solution of EDTA and eggshell powder under microwave heating. The formed material had a weak fluorescence intensity and bad excitation and emission spectra shape which were not even a mirror image to each other. As previously reported, the emission of weak luminous subfluorophores could be enhanced with appropriate immobilization, like covalent bonding, cross-linking or physical immobilization [20]. To improve the fluorescence emission of the above CQDs, we hypothesized that an appropriate chemical addition was needed to assemble a more cross-linked strict core structure of CQDs. The polyhydroxy compound ‘’glycerol’ was used as a diluent instead of water. Glycerol worked as a crosslinking additive to fabricate luminescent eggshell@CQDs under the same conditions [21]. After the addition of glycerol, an orange solution of fluorescent eggshell@CQDs was formed within only 90 s with an acceptable quantum yield of 8.1 %. It was confirmed that glycerol itself could not produce fluorescent CQDs under the same microwave heating circumstances. Each component alone in glycerol was treated with the same conditions to study the role of eggshell in the preparation of CQDs. Fig. 2 shows that the presence of eggshell enhances the fluorescence yield additively with EDTA. This may be explained by the multi-doping strategy as an eggshell composed of several metals such as sulfur, nitrogen, calcium, and phosphorus [18].

High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) images (Fig. 3
A) show uniform dispersion of eggshell@CQDs with uniform sizes varying from 2 to 7 nm with an average diameter of 5.5 nm. The eggshell@CQDs were found to be amorphous as indicated by the pattern viewed in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image (inset of Fig. 3 A). The prepared eggshell@CQDs display a fluorescence emission peak at 408 nm after excitation at 340 nm ( Fig. S1 ). It was found that the emission wavelength and intensity are dependent on the excitation wavelength. After switching the excitation wavelength over a range of 280–380 nm, the best one was selected at 340 nm ( Fig. S2 ). FTIR was used to characterize the chemical structure of the prepared eggshell@CQDs. The spectrum (Fig. 3 B) showed a broad band at 3413 cm−1 which could be assigned to -O-H stretching. Moreover, the bands centered in 2933, 2127, 1647,1413, 1332, 1111, 1043 and 855 cm−1 could be attributed to C-H, S-H, C = O, C-N, S = O, C-S-C, P-O-R, and Ca stretching respectively [19,22–25]. This observation supported the existence of carboxylic, sulfhydryl, hydroxyl, and phosphate moieties in the eggshell@CQDs. The surface components of the eggshell@CQDs were further examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full survey spectrum was presented in Fig. 3 C with values of 286.53 eV (C1s), 534.31 eV (O1s), 170.70 eV (S2p), 401.96 eV (N1s), 128.71 eV (P2p), and 350.17 eV (Ca2p). The synthesized eggshell@CQDs mainly contain C (69.98 %), O (26 %), S (2.6 %), with incorporation of N (0.84 %), P (0.29 %) and Ca (0.28 %). C1s spectrum (Fig. S3 A) represents three main crests with values of 285.29 eV (C-N), 287.52 eV (C = O), and 289.23 eV (COOH) [26,27]. O1s spectrum (Fig. S3 B) represents three main crests with values of 531.48 eV (C = O), 532.35 eV (C-O-C), and 533.50 eV (C-OH) [28–30]. S2p spectrum (Fig. S3 C) represents three main crests with values of 164.92 eV (S-C 2p1/2), 169.1 eV (S-O 2p1/2), and 169.61 eV (S = O) [29,31]. N1s spectrum (Fig. S3 D) represents two crests with values of 400.12 eV (pyrrolic N), and 402.56 eV (C3-N) [32,33]. P2p spectrum (Fig. S3 E) represents two crests with values of 134.63 eV (P-C/P-N)[34], and the other crest of 138.07 eV is reported for phosphorus in high oxidation states, indicating surface oxidized species [35]. This may be owing to chemical changes in the dopants' conditions during material production. Ca2p spectrum (Fig. S3 F) represents two crests with values of 347.06 eV (Ca2p3/2), and 350.29 eV (Ca2p1/2) [36]. The XPS analysis confirms the presence of consistent groups identified through the FT-IR spectra.

The blue fluorescence of eggshell@CQDs was significantly quenched in the presence of molnupiravir. Increasing the concentration of molnupiravir turned off the fluorescence of the eggshell@CQDs quantitatively (Fig. 4 ). Generally, there are three main categories of fluorescence quenching mechanisms, static quenching, dynamic quenching and inner filter effect (IFE). As no overlapping between the absorption spectrum of molnupiravir and the excitation or emission spectra of the eggshell@CQDs, IFE was excluded [37]. Dynamic and static quenching mechanisms could be differentiated by lifetime observations or their temperature dependency. For dynamic quenching, increasing the temperature leads to faster diffusion and increases the Stern-Volmer quenching constant(Ksv), but in static quenching, increasing the temperature leads to dissociation of the formed complex and decreases the Stern-Volmer quenching constant(Ksv) [38]. To conclude the possible quenching mechanism, the Stern-Volmer equation was engaged.
F °
F
= 1 + K s v
Q
= 1 + K q τ 0
Q
As F° and F refer to the fluorescence emission intensities in case of the absence and presence of molnupiravir, respectively; Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant; [Q] refers to the concentration of molnupiravir. Kq refers to the quenching rate constant, and τ0 refers to the average lifetime (10−8 s). Stern–Volmer plots were studied at three different temperatures (298, 308 and 318 K) as shown in Fig. S4
. It was noted that the values of Ksv decreased consequently upon increasing the temperature, confirming the possibility of a static quenching mechanism. Furthermore, Kq values were calculated from the observed Ksv values and were found to be 3.1x1011, 1.8x1011, and 1.6x1011 L mol−1 s−1 which are larger than the maximum diffusion rate constant (2.0 × 1010L mol−1s−1), verifying the static mechanism of quenching [39]. Fig. S5 showed changes in eggshell@CQDs UV/Vis spectrum after the addition of molnupiravir as a new absorption peak appeared at 240 nm, referring to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction between the surface function groups of molnupiravir and the multi-doped eggshell@CQDs, inducing static quenching.
To reach the highest possible sensitivity of the synthesized eggshell@CQDs, some experimental factors such as volume of eggshell@CQDs, pH, and incubation time were investigated and optimized.

The reaction between eggshell@CQDs and molnupiravir was validated using the guidelines of the International Council for Harmonization (ICH) Q2 R1[42]. The fluorescence quenching of eggshell@CQDs after the addition of molnupiravir was recorded at 408 nm after being excited at 340 nm.

To construct the calibration curve, the quenching values (F°-F) were plotted against the corresponding concentrations of molnupiravir in µg/mL. A good correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9985 was obtained in the concentration range of 2.5 – 70 µg/mL (inset of Fig. 4). The detection limit (DL) was calculated from the mathematical equation; DL = 3.3 × SD/slope, as SD is the standard deviation of the intercept. DL was found to be 0.8 µg/mL. Table S1 illustrates the analytical merits of the designed methodology.

To assess the accuracy of the proposed approach, the average %recoveries of triplicate determination of three levels of concentration (5, 30, 50 µg/mL) of molnupiravir were calculated. The calculated %recoveries ranged from 99.96 to 101.79 % highlighting the accuracy of the approach. At the same time, the intra and inter-day precision were stated as %RSD that was found to be less than 0.91 proving the reproducibility of the designed approach (Table 1 ).

To examine the robustness of the suggested approach, the influence of slight deviations in the experimental variables related to molnupiravir fluorescence sensing was observed. These variables are the excitation wavelength (340 nm ± 2) and the volume of eggshell@CQDs (200 µL ± 2). As shown in Table S2 , it was proved that minor modifications had no noticeable impact on the quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of molnupiravir in pharmaceutical capsules
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed methodology was utilized to quantify molnupiravir in Molnupiravir Eva Pharma® hard capsules. The average percentage recoveries and % RSD (Relative Standard Deviation) were calculated. Additionally, the capsules were analyzed using the established HPLC methodology [8], as depicted in Fig. S11 . The t-test and F-test were employed to statistically compare the two methods, and the results indicated that there were no significant differences at the 95 % confidence level (Table 2 ). This suggests that the developed spectrofluorometric method is acceptable for detecting molnupiravir in commercial capsules. While the HPLC/UV approach is effective in molnupiravir determination, it has several drawbacks, including high costs, labour-intensive processes, long processing times, significant solvent consumption, and the need for skilled workers. In contrast, the designed spectrofluorometric approach is inexpensive, quick, user-friendly, and easy to handle.

The suggested approach was evaluated and compared with previously described methods. As shown in Table 3 , the linearity range and detection limit of the suggested methodology are comparable to the published methods. Moreover, the employed technique did not require costly equipment, such as those used in HPLC and LC-MS/MS techniques [8,43], and no need for a prior reaction [44] or complicated mathematical models [45]. Furthermore, the sample throughput of the spectrofluorometric analysis is substantially high compared with chromatographic procedures that require time for the elution of the examined analytes [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Greenness evaluation and comparison of the developed approach to the previously reported HPLC approach

ce:section.ce:para: Green, fluorescent eggshell@CQDs were fabricated and applied as a nano-probe for the accurate quantitative estimation of molnupiravir without requiring prior derivatization or pH adjustment. The eggshell@CQDs were prepared from inexpensive, abundant, environmentally friendly, and readily available starting materials in a quick one-step microwave heating process for 90 s. The prepared eggshell@CQDs were characterized using field emission TEM, FT-IR, and XPS ensuring multi-doping of the CQDs. The fluorescence emission of the prepared eggshell@CQDs was quantitatively quenched in the presence of molnupiravir based on a static mechanism. The quenching process was optimized in words of volume of eggshell@CQDs, pH, and incubation time. A good linearity was obtained over a wide concentration range, with a low detection limit. Furthermore, a systematic assessment of the greenness of the suggested method was performed, and the findings showed that, in contrast to the previously published HPLC approach, the suggested method had the least environmental impact. The new approach demonstrated satisfactory selectivity, accuracy, and repeatability for the assay of molnupiravir in pharmaceutical preparations.

Reham E. Kannouma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Amira H. Kamal: . Mohamed A. Hammad: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Fotouh R. Mansour: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the Main Chemical Lab (Cairo, Egypt) for their assistance with the characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110397.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Abdel-Megied, A validated LC-MS/MS method for determination of antiviral prodrug molnupiravir in human plasma and its application for a pharmacokinetic modeling study in healthy Egyptian volunteers, J. Chromatogr. B. 1206 (2022) 123363. doi: 10.1016/j.jchromb.2022.123363.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The development and validation of a novel LC-MS/MS method for the simultaneous quantification of molnupiravir and its metabolite ß-d-N4-hydroxycytidine in human plasma and saliva
